data.description:

The outlook for 2026 is positive, with continued focus on AI, product innovation, and disciplined capital allocation.

data.title: ICE Canola Correcting Lower in Early Trading
data.description:
WINNIPEG, Manitoba--ICE canola futures were weaker Friday morning, taking back some of Thursday's advances as contracts consolidated just under nearby highs.

Intermonth spreading remained a feature, with participants rolling out of the March contract and into the May. The May contract settled above its 200-day moving average on Thursday but was back below that chart level Friday morning.

Losses in Chicago soybeans and soyoil accounted for some spillover selling in canola. European rapeseed and Malaysian palm oil were narrowly mixed.

Canada exported 185,800 metric tons of canola during the week ended Feb. 8, which was up 41% from the previous week. However, crop-year-to-date exports of 3.77 million metric tons remain well behind the 5.67 million metric tons exported by the same time the previous year.

North American futures markets will be closed Monday for holidays in Canada and the U.S.

About 23,500 canola contracts had traded as of 9:38 a.m. EST.

Prices in Canadian dollars per metric tonne at 9:38 a.m. EST:

 
           Price      Change 
Mar       662.70     dn 5.10 
May       674.40     dn 4.80 
Jul       682.90     dn 5.20 
Nov       676.00     dn 4.00

Source: Commodity News Service Canada, news@marketsfarm.com

data.title: REG - Bank of Nova Scotia - Final Terms
data.description:
RNS Number : 0102T Bank of Nova Scotia 13 February 2026  

RNS ANNOUNCEMENT

The Bank of Nova Scotia

13 February 2026

Publication of Final Terms

Not for release, publication or distribution, directly or indirectly, in or into the United States.

The Final Terms dated 11 February 2026 in respect of the issue by The Bank of Nova Scotia (the "Issuer") of USD 50,000,000 Callable 4.34 per cent. Senior Notes due February 2032 (the "Notes") pursuant to its Euro Medium Term Note Programme (the "Programme") are available for viewing.

The Final Terms in respect of the Notes must be read in conjunction with the Prospectus dated 8 July 2026 and the supplemental prospectuses dated 26 August 2025 and 11 December 2025 relating to the Programme (together, the "Prospectus") which constitutes a base prospectus for the purposes of the Prospectus Regulation (Regulation (EU) 2017/1129, as amended), as it forms part of United Kingdom domestic law by virtue of the European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018, in order to obtain all the relevant information.  Full information on the Issuer and the offer of the Notes is only available on the basis of the combination of the Final Terms and the Prospectus.

To view the Final Terms in respect of the Notes, please paste the following URL into the address bar of your browser: http://www.rns-pdf.londonstockexchange.com/rns/0102T_1-2026-2-13.pdf

DISCLAIMER - INTENDED ADDRESSEES

Please note that the information contained in the Prospectus and the Final Terms, may be addressed to and/or targeted at persons who are residents of particular countries (specified in the Prospectus) only and is not intended for use and should not be relied upon by any person outside these countries and/or to whom the offer contained in the Final Terms is not addressed.  Prior to relying on the information contained in the Final Terms you must ascertain from the Prospectus, as supplemented by the Final Terms, whether or not you are part of the intended addressees of the information contained therein.

The Notes have not been and will not be registered under the United States Securities Act of 1933, as amended (the "Securities Act").  Subject to certain exceptions, the Notes may not be offered, sold or delivered in or into the United States or to or for the account or benefit of US persons (as defined in Regulation S under the Securities Act).  No public offering of the Notes is being made in the United States.

No PRIIPs or UK PRIIPs key information document (KID) has been prepared as not available to retail in EEA or UK.

data.title: Goldman Sachs CEO tells CNBC he 'reluctantly accepted' lawyer Ruemmler's resignation
data.description:
Goldman Sachs CEO David Solomon said he had reluctantly accepted Chief Legal Officer Kathy Ruemmler's resignation, he told CNBC on Friday and stressed that he respected her decision to leave.

"I reluctantly accepted her resignation, but I respect her decision," he told CNBC in an interview.

Recent documents released by the U.S. Justice Department showed Ruemmler accepted gifts from late sex offender Jeffrey Epstein and advised him on how to address media inquiries regarding his crimes.

The departure of Ruemmler, who was among the top executive officers of the Wall Street firm, is the most high-profile banking exit after the release of the latest Epstein documents.

Solomon told CNBC Ruemmler called him on Thursday afternoon and told him that the press coverage of the work that she had done previously had "just gotten to a level of noise and distraction that she thought was distracting the firm."

"It was putting her in a position where it was hard for her to execute on her job and her responsibilities, and she just thought it was time to step away," Solomon said.

Ruemmler had a large number of communications with Epstein from 2014 to 2019, even after the disgraced financier's 2008 guilty plea for procuring a person under the age of 18 for prostitution, the documents showed.

Before joining Goldman Sachs in 2020, Ruemmler was global chair of the white collar defense and investigations practice at Latham & Watkins LLP.

Ruemmler, who was also White House counsel during the Obama administration, referred to Epstein in emails as "Uncle Jeffrey" and received gifts from him including wine and a handbag, the documents show.

Epstein also called Ruemmler's cell phone when he was arrested on July 6, 2019, among other calls he made that night, according to two documents that cited notes from law enforcement officials.

Ruemmler could not be reached for a comment.

Goldman backed the lawyer during the release of the several documents by the U.S. Justice Department, with Solomon saying on Thursday in a statement she was one of the most accomplished professionals in her field.

On Friday, he told CNBC, Goldman's management team worked very closely with Ruemmler over the last six years.

"We know her as a professional and we know her as a human being. And I think that's lost sometimes in some of the dialogue," Solomon said on CNBC. "I'm disappointed that it got to this."

data.title: Timken Declares Quarterly Dividend of 35 Cents Per Share
data.description:
NORTH CANTON, Ohio, Feb. 13, 2026 /PRNewswire/ — The board of directors of The Timken Company (; www.timken.com), a global technology leader in engineered bearings and industrial motion, today declared a quarterly cash dividend of 35 cents per share. The dividend is payable on March 6, 2026, to shareholders of record as of Feb. 24, 2026.

Timken has paid a dividend on its common shares every quarter since its original listing on the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE) in 1922. The upcoming dividend represents 415 consecutive quarters, one of the longest-running dividend streaks among NYSE-listed companies.

About The Timken Company

The Timken Company (; www.timken.com), a global technology leader in engineered bearings and industrial motion, designs a growing portfolio of next-generation products for diverse industries. For more than 125 years, Timken has used its specialized expertise to innovate and create customer-centric solutions that increase reliability and efficiency. Timken posted $4.6 billion in sales in 2025 and employs approximately 19,000 people globally, operating from 45 countries.

Media Relations:

Scott Schroeder

234.262.6420

scott.schroeder@timken.com

Investor Relations:

Neil Frohnapple

234.262.2310

investors@timken.com

View original content to download multimedia:https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/timken-declares-quarterly-dividend-of-35-cents-per-share-302687726.html

SOURCE The Timken Company

data.title: REG - React Group PLC - Director/PDMR Shareholding
data.description:
RNS Number : 0089T React Group PLC 13 February 2026  

13 February 2026

REACT Group plc

("REACT", the "Group" or the "Company")

Director/PDMR Shareholding

As announced on 16 July 2025, the REACT Share Incentive Plan ("SIP") is an HMRC sanctioned plan under which eligible participating employees of the Company can elect to purchase ordinary shares of 12.5 pence each in the Company ("Ordinary Shares"), via MUFG Corporate Markets ("MUFG") as administrator and trustee to the SIP, using monthly salary deductions. Ordinary Shares acquired in this manner are referred to as "Partnership Shares" and, for every two Partnership Shares Purchased, participants are awarded three further Ordinary Shares, known as a "Matching Shares", at nil cost.

On 13 February 2026, REACT received notification from MUFG that, on 12 February 2026, it purchased an aggregate of 852 Partnership Shares in the market at a price of 52.75 pence per Ordinary Share on behalf of those Directors / PDMRs set out in the table below. At the same time, these individuals were also awarded (for no consideration) their corresponding Matching Shares.

Director / PDMR

Tel: +44 (0) 207 496 3000

Philip Davies / Alex Bond

About Us:

REACT Group Plc, the UK's leading support services provider to the facilities management (FM) sector, operates through four distinct divisions:

·      LaddersFree, one of the UK's largest commercial window cleaning businesses, delivering nationwide services.

·      Fidelis Contract Services ("Fidelis"), a contract cleaning and soft facilities maintenance provider focused on long-term customer partnerships.

·      REACT, specialising in emergency and specialist cleaning solutions offering both long-term framework agreements and rapid response services.

·      24hr Aquaflow Services, a recently acquired commercial drainage and plumbing business serving customers across the South East of England

This diversified structure enables REACT Group to offer a comprehensive suite of essential and time-sensitive services, supporting both recurring maintenance and urgent response needs across the UK.

data.title: Crypto Falls Back to Earth — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Marcello Estevão

About the author: Marcello Estevão is managing director and chief economist at the Institute of International Finance and a professor at Georgetown University.

One shift in the markets is becoming increasingly difficult to ignore. Assets long associated with capital preservation, such as gold and silver, are moving steadily higher. Cryptocurrencies, which for years claimed a similar role, are headed in the opposite direction.

In 2025, Bitcoin and Ethereum delivered negative returns, and the broader crypto market lost roughly $1.3 trillion in capitalization in the fourth quarter. The new year has done little to alter that trajectory. Crypto prices continued to weaken in January even as metals rallied, with brief interruptions driven by technical adjustments rather than renewed conviction.

Crypto and precious metals compete for the same conceptual space. Both have been framed as protection against currency debasement, fiscal excess, and institutional fragility. When capital flows decisively toward one and away from the other, it usually reflects a deeper reassessment rather than a passing mood.

Part of this can be explained by changes in global liquidity. In the U.S., years of quantitative tightening reduced the pool of excess capital that once sustained speculative enthusiasm. At the same time, a powerful new claimant on financial resources has emerged: Hyperscalers and software companies, which for years generated abundant free cash flow, have become heavy consumers of capital. The race to build AI infrastructure has absorbed hundreds of billions of dollars that might otherwise have been returned to shareholders and indirectly supported demand for higher-risk assets.

Across Asia, the picture looks different. In China, money growth exceeds nominal gross domestic product growth by a wide margin. With crypto trading and mining effectively prohibited, domestic liquidity has sought other outlets. Precious metals have benefited disproportionately, supported by regional trade surpluses and reinforced by a steady redirection of capital toward tangible stores of value. The result has been a gradual but persistent shift in global flows from West to East and from digital abstractions to physical assets.

Liquidity doesn't tell the full story. Attention matters as well. When Bitcoin was created 17 years ago, crypto captured the public's imagination as a new financial frontier, where innovation and speculative excitement converged. It promised a rethinking of money and a parallel financial system immune to political discretion.

That promise remains largely unrealized. Crypto is costly and inefficient as a means of payment and plays no meaningful role in the day-to-day functioning of the global economy. Even where adoption was actively promoted, results were modest. Stablecoins have found niche uses, but they make up only a small share of the crypto ecosystem and do little to justify the valuations of its core assets.

Meanwhile, the center of innovation has moved on. AI, electrification, and infrastructure investment now dominate investors' imagination. In markets, novelty has a half-life. Crypto now must fight not only against skepticism, but against alternative assets that offer both excitement and economic relevance.

This re-examination highlights crypto's key vulnerability. Unlike equities, bonds, or real assets, crypto generates no cash flows, confers no legal claims, and rests on no institutional backstop. Its value depends almost entirely on collective belief. That belief can flourish in periods of abundant liquidity and low opportunity cost, but it becomes far more fragile when capital tightens and investors begin to ask harder questions.

Recent market behavior has been revealing. At a time of elevated geopolitical tension, fiscal uncertainty, and concern about currency debasement — conditions under which crypto should in theory perform well — investors have favored gold instead. Crypto has behaved less like a haven and more like a speculative asset whose narrative has lost momentum.

A new consideration, quantum computing, has also disrupted the crypto picture. The security of major blockchains rests on cryptographic methods that assume limits to classical computing power. Quantum computing challenges those assumptions by design. While advances in quantum computing don't pose an immediate threat to crypto, they do introduce an uncertainty that markets can't dismiss.

Crypto's value depends on the perception that the underlying system is fundamentally secure. There is no alternative foundation if that perception is meaningfully challenged. Even a distant and low-probability risk carries disproportionate weight when the downside is existential.

By contrast, physical assets like gold don't depend on protocols, updates, or computational assumptions. Their role as stores of value is technologically neutral. In a world where technology is an increasingly significant source of uncertainty, gold's neutrality has gained in value.

Political dynamics add another layer of volatility. Over the past two years, crypto benefited from unusually strong political tailwinds in the U.S. Explicit support from leading politicians translated into sharp price gains. That support proved fleeting. Much of the post-2024 election surge has reversed, proving that political enthusiasm can amplify momentum, but it rarely sustains value on its own.

Crypto isn't finished. But the market has entered a new phase. As liquidity tightens, attention shifts, and novel risks are introduced, capital becomes more selective. When that happens, assets sustained primarily by narrative lose gravitational pull.

Markets usually signal these changes quietly, but over the past few months, the signals have been quite clear. The center of gravity has shifted, and crypto is falling back to Earth.

Guest commentaries like this one are written by authors outside the Barron's newsroom. They reflect the perspective and opinions of the authors. Submit feedback and commentary pitches to ideas@barrons.com .

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.
